title: God's Lamb
speaker: Pastor Chris Lensch
source_files: God's Lamb - GUEST SPEAKER_ Pastor Chris Lensch - John 1.txt

outline.point: I. The Lamb Introduced: John the Baptist Identifies Jesus
subpoints: A. John's role as herald pointing to Christ (John 1:29)
B. The Lamb who takes away the sin of the world
C. The Spirit's sign confirming the Lamb's identity

outline.point: II. The Lamb's Qualifications: God's Own Son as Sacrifice
subpoints: B. Of divine worth—eternally in the bosom of the Father
C. The High Priest who offered himself (Hebrews 7:24-27)

outline.point: III. The Lamb's Work: Taking Away Sin and Giving Life
subpoints: A. The Lamb's death forgives sin and breaks death's power
B. The resurrection as vindication of the sacrifice
C. Assurance through the Spirit's witness

outline.point: IV. The Lamb Glorified: Revelation 5 and the Heavenly Throne
subpoints: A. The scroll sealed with seven seals—only the Lamb is worthy
B. The Lion of Judah appears as the Lamb who was slain
C. Universal worship of the Lamb by all creation

outline.point: V. The Wrath of the Lamb and the End of History
subpoints: A. Kings hiding from the Lamb's wrath (Revelation 6)
B. Armageddon and the two suppers of Revelation 19
C. The wedding supper vs. the birds—two eternal destinies

outline.point: VI. Our Response: Trust in the Lamb
subpoints: A. Hope in this life through the risen, interceding Lamb
B. Eternal life and reigning with him
C. Call to receive the Lamb who takes away sin

summary:
Guest speaker Pastor Chris Lensch traces the identity of Jesus as God's Lamb from John the Baptist's proclamation in John 1 through the heavenly visions of Revelation. Beginning with John's declaration 'Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world,' he shows that Jesus is uniquely qualified as both perfect man and eternal Son of God to serve as the final high priest who offers himself as the atoning sacrifice. The sermon weaves together the Lamb's humility in his first coming with his exaltation as the one worthy to open the scroll of judgment.

Drawing from Revelation 5, 6, and 19, Lensch presents the full arc of the Lamb's story: from humble sacrifice to wrathful judge and joyful bridegroom. Those who spurn God's Lamb will face his wrath at history's end, while those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb will reign with him forever. The sermon closes with the encouragement that the risen Lamb intercedes for his people now and will receive them to himself at his return.
transcript:
I'll be referencing the Revelation chapter 5 passage toward the end of the message, but to begin with I'd like to have us turn to John chapter 1, where the Lamb is introduced by his herald. John the Baptist introduces Jesus in John 1 as the Lamb of God.

## The Lamb Introduced in John 1

John the Baptist had his own disciples, and down in the Jordan valley, the next day John saw Jesus coming toward him — this is after the baptism of Jesus already. And John said, "Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. This is he of whom I said, after me comes a man who is preferred before me, for he was before me. I did not know him, but that he should be revealed to Israel, therefore I came baptizing with water." So John says his whole mission was about the Messiah who was coming; otherwise John wouldn't have come.

In verse 32, John bore witness saying, "I saw the Spirit descending from heaven like a dove and he remained upon him. I did not know him, but he who sent me to baptize with water said to me, upon whom you see the Spirit descending and remaining on him, this is he who baptizes with the Holy Spirit." And, "I have seen and testified that this is the Son of God."

And then in verse 29, a couple days later, John says again, "Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world."

The passage opens with the Lamb, and everybody who was Jewish knew about sacrifices in the temple — how you bring a gift to God, bring a lamb to confess that you are a debtor to God for your life. You take something innocent and undefiled and bring that as the gift. They would lay their hands upon the lamb and say, "Take away my sins. I recognize that I am a sinner. I am in debt to God for the life that he gives to me." But then this passage closes with, "Behold him, he is the very Son of God." He's God's Lamb, but he's also God's Son.

There's a lot of theology of identity around who this Jesus of Nazareth really is. There was a recent TV show called The Masked Singer that was in the news because one of their singers had been performing behind a mask, and they said, well, it's not too bad — but when they took the mask off, it was Rudy Giuliani. It was controversial because two of the judges didn't like Rudy's politics and so they stormed off the stage. But they were okay with it as long as he was masked. And that's the way Israel took Jesus of Nazareth — okay as long as we don't call him the Lamb of God, okay as long as we don't call him the eternal Son of God. We see that picture in verse 11 of John 1: "He came to his own, and his own received him not." As the identity of Jesus is proclaimed more and more as the promised one, more and more he is going to be rejected.

John does not introduce Jesus as the expected king of Israel. But John — who says in Matthew 3, "You come to me, I should be baptized by you" — recognizes from the very beginning that this is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. John the great, the final prophet of God, says here who he is: "I'm the one pointing the spotlight. I'm the one with a megaphone saying it's not me you're looking for — you're looking for God's Lamb who takes away the sin of the world."

But just who is this Lamb who can take away sin — sin that brings death, ruin, destruction? It is the same one who in Mark 2 raises up the lame man who had not walked in years and says, "So that you may know that the Son of Man has power on earth to forgive sins — I say to you, take up your bed and walk." This is the Lamb of God, the same one who has power to forgive sins. And if he can forgive sins, he can also deliver us from death, because death is the result — the wages of sin.

That's who the Lamb is. He's the eternal Son of God, as John says in verse 34. In verse 18 of John 1, he is the one who has always and forever been in the bosom of the Father, and yet he comes — born into our world, born into our human family — so that he might be the Lamb to take away the sin of the world. He's the Lamb who has the ultimate power, part of God's plan to not only take away sin, but in taking away sin, to give us life.

Verse 4 of John 1: "In him was life, and the life was the light of men." John writes again later in 1 John 1, "God is light, and in him is no darkness at all." Jesus is identified as God — he was the Word, and the Word was with God, and more than that, the Word was God, because he'd always been there as God. He is the one who brings life, because he is the light of men. Life comes only when sin is taken away by the Lamb of God.

## The Lamb as High Priest

Think about the baptism. When I was growing up I went to a Lutheran camp, and the question was asked, why do you think Jesus was baptized? For Lutherans, the big focus of baptism is the washing away of sins, and we believe that too — it does signify that and gives a new beginning. But Jesus is baptized also so that at his baptism the Holy Spirit in the form of a dove comes and rests upon him. That's the picture of God himself, God the Spirit coming to rest upon him, to say that he is the anointed one.

Jesus is standing in the Jordan River, and we believe that's when he begins his public ministry. His ministry covers three offices: prophet, priest, and king. But John the Baptist is particularly focusing upon his office as priest, because he's the one who is going to take away the sins of the world. That's what the high priest would do. Every Yom Kippur, every Day of Atonement, the high priest would once a year offer a special sacrifice to cover the sins of the people. Jesus is recognized in his anointing by God's Spirit, and also by his baptism by John publicly, that now he is beginning his ministry. As the great high priest, he came to take away the sin of the world.

When we look at the description of Jesus's priesthood in the book of Hebrews, let me read from chapter 7, verses 24 through 27:

> *"Therefore he is also able to save to the uttermost those who come to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them. For such a high priest was fitting for us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and has become higher than the heavens; who does not need daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifices, first for his own sins and then for the people's. For this he did once for all when he offered up himself."* (Hebrews 7:25-27)

There is the Lamb of God offering himself. There is the high priest who has been anointed to offer the sacrifice. And yet why did God even become the God-man? Because he needed a body to represent humanity, and he needed a body to offer up as a sacrifice pleasing to God. Hebrews 10:4 says that a body God prepared for him. He offered himself. Jesus's power is not so much in his office of the high priesthood as it is in the very sacrifice that he offered as high priest, and that sacrifice was his own body.

As God's Lamb, Jesus was without blemish. He was a man — a perfect man — to save you and me, imperfect sinners. But more than a perfect man, he was God's Son, God's Lamb of divine worth because he was divine, who had always been in the bosom of the Father until he came to reveal him to us.

## How John Knew: The Spirit's Witness

How did John know that Jesus was this Lamb, that Jesus would be the final sacrifice? Twice in John 1, he says, "I did not know him" — verse 31 and verse 33. And yet John and Jesus were relatives. It seems like Jesus's mother Mary was some kind of relation of Elizabeth, the mother of John. We remember that story from Luke 1, when Mary came to visit Elizabeth. After that, these two sons — both specially sent by God into the world — apparently never met until the public ministry of Jesus began.

Here is how John knew, from the end of verse 33: "I did not know him, but he who sent me to baptize with water said to me, upon whom you see the Spirit descending and remaining on him, this is he who baptizes with the Holy Spirit." John was given a sign. And who told John about the sign? The same Spirit that comes upon Jesus at his baptism — that same Spirit spoke, either in John's mind or in his ear: this is the one you're looking for. John had baptized a lot of people down in the Jordan River, but nothing like this had ever happened — the Spirit of God coming down upon one of those baptized. And so he knew.

John might have known from his mother, who told John as a lad, "Before you were born, you were a special child. We never expected to have children, and yet God gave you to us." That's what the name John means — a gift of God. She may have told him. But why did John as a preborn baby leap in his mother's womb? Because Mary came and brought the just-incarnate Son of God into the very same presence.

And there may have been other witnesses in young John's life. His father was a priest, and his father might have said, "I heard stories after I finished serving in the temple. There was a Simeon and there was an Anna, and they were saying that the promised one had come and they actually beheld him." So John from early on may have been anticipating this, but he never knew until the Spirit of God gave direct revelation to John the Baptist: this is the one you are looking for.

God can speak that way today. We don't know that God speaks audibly to his people today, though it may happen on occasion. But we do know that the Spirit of God still impresses himself upon us. We know that from 1 Corinthians 2:11-12: "No one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God. Now we have received not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God — so that we might know the things that have been freely given to us by God." God wants us to know him and know what he has in store for us. One of the greatest gifts God gives us is his Word, revealed by the Spirit, with all the promises and plans of God laid out. The Spirit still works in the lives of people to illuminate them — especially from his Word, but also through impulses and leadings.

The things of God are spiritually discerned, and without that Spirit we cannot know God. But the Spirit of God is totally distinct from the spirit of the world. So John the Baptist said, "This is the one — the Lamb of God that takes away the sin of the world."

## The End for John the Baptist

This is not the end of John's relation to Jesus. Jesus actually gains one or two disciples from John the Baptist, and John carries on — but John is going to begin to diminish and Jesus begins to advance in his public preaching and ministry, as he should.

The end for John the Baptist comes in a jail down by the Dead Sea. Herod Antipas had built a great fortress there on the east side of the Jordan, and that's where John will die. Before he dies, he's in jail and he sends some disciples to Jesus, saying in Matthew 11:2-3, "Are you the one?" I don't think John is so much doubting Jesus as the one — given that great profession in John 1 that he's the Son of God, the Lamb of God. I think John is doubting himself. He expected greater things. He thought Jesus might set up his throne, or he thought, "If he's healed other people and delivered them from demonic oppression, he could deliver me from Herod Antipas. Herod's got me locked up here." He might have been doubting himself or confused about what to look for.

Jesus doesn't rebuke John. He sends a message back: "You don't have to tell him if I'm the one — just tell him what my ministry has been, that the lame are walking, the deaf are hearing, and the poor are having the gospel preached to them. Tell him that." And that's all it would take. John the Baptist could die in peace.

Jesus gives us assurance in the same way. We see it through the promises, but the promises have to be ours before we can draw assurance from them. And we also see it through other things the Spirit of God is doing. We keep praying, and a prayer is answered. God answers and says, "I haven't left you." We have an unexpected friendship in a time of need — someone calls, someone stops by. Surprising conversions — we never expected that person. With God, nothing is impossible. God can work in any life he wants to, and those are the assurances God gives us that he is still keeping his word, keeping his promises, keeping close to us, and giving us life.

We thank God for his grace to prepare and call a person like John the Baptist. It was a divine mercy to that generation to have a herald go out and sound the trumpet: it's time. Get ready to meet your King. He is the eternal Son of God. He's going to give you life. But first, before he gives you life, he's going to take away the sin of the world. And that reaches out beyond Israel — it's not just our sin, it's the sin of the world, to include people like us beyond the originally chosen nation.

## The Lamb Glorified: Revelation 5

The Apostle John has more to say about the Lamb, and we read that in Revelation 5. Let me turn there to focus on what the book of Revelation says about the Lamb of God.

The book of Revelation focuses quite a bit on the end of the world. The message of Revelation is that the world is in trouble — there are judgments that begin to come, unfold, get more intense, rolling one into the next. But the ancient title of the book is the Revelation of Jesus Christ. In the first chapter we see the exalted, glorified Christ — not the humble Jesus, but the glorified God who has been sitting at the right hand of the Father. Now Jesus has his day. It is the revelation of Christ, and it is the end of history. Christ is coming back in power.

Right now we're waiting for the end of history. It seems like sinners have their day. All through history since Jesus came the first time, God appears to be silent sometimes, and sinners seem to be having their day. But God says, "When I have reaped the harvest and gathered in all the elect, then ultimately I will have my day" — and that day is called the Day of the Lord. That's the book of Revelation.

In chapter 5, John has been caught up to heaven. Some things are beyond words for him. But he hears intelligible voices, and in verse 2 he says, "I saw a strong angel proclaiming with a loud voice, 'Who is worthy to open the scroll and to loose its seals?'" It's a sealed scroll with seven seals on it. You have to have the proper authority to break those seals, because those seals are put there for a reason. This is the scroll containing the six and then seven judgments, which will be opened up as Christ opens the scroll — and the judgments begin to take place in chapter 6.

John is observing all this and gets engaged in it, even somewhat passionate, and he starts crying in verse 4. One of the elders says in verse 5, "Do not weep. Behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has prevailed to open the scroll and to loose its seven seals." Bring in the Lion! It's the Day of the Lord. The judgments will begin to fall upon everyone who has been fighting against God. This is the beginning of the end when this scroll is opened.

But who is the Lion? In verse 8, when he had taken the scroll, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down, and they sing in verse 9, "You are worthy to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain and have redeemed us to God by your blood, out of every tribe and tongue and people and nation, and you have made us kings and priests to our God and we shall reign upon the earth." And in verse 12: "Worthy is the Lamb who was slain."

We were looking for a Lion, but it's the Lamb who is next to the throne — who stands up and takes the scroll and says, it's time. This is the beginning of the end, the end of history, as God has his day to bring justice upon all who fought against him. People are rejoicing on that day. We can rejoice today too, knowing that God is sovereign, that he's going to even the scales of justice. Man has had his day; God is going to make everything right at the end of the age.

The wonderful thing is that God is ransoming a people out of every tribe and tongue and nation — that's verse 9. The redemption is through the blood of the Lamb who was slain. God is satisfied in his justice that this Lamb represented sinners like you and me. Verse 10 is the positive verse: the redeemed will have the role of priest-kings and will reign upon the earth — a beautiful picture of what our salvation means.

## The Wrath of the Lamb

As the Day of the Lord begins — turning to chapter 6 — there are earthquakes and troubles in the earth, and with these judgments the kings of the earth, the great men, the rich men, the commanders, the mighty, and the common people all hide themselves in the caves and in the rocks of the mountains, saying to the mountains and rocks, "Fall on us and hide us" — they would rather be buried, they can't take much more of these judgments. Why? Verse 16: "to hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb."

Has anyone ever seen an angry lamb? We might have seen an angry ram, but never an angry lamb. But here is the identity that John the Baptist introduced: Jesus willingly and lovingly gives himself as the sacrifice, but that sacrifice is not to be spurned or despised. Those who despise the sacrifice of Christ for a sinful people will suffer the consequences of turning their back on God's Son. The wrath of the Lamb. He alone has the authority to judge sinners — those made in the image of God but fallen and corrupted.

## Two Suppers in Revelation 19

Finally we turn to Revelation 19. This is Armageddon — the final battle of the rebellion against God in history. Are you aware that there are two suppers in chapter 19? There is the supper of the Lamb, the bridal supper. And then there is another supper, in verse 17 — God calls the birds: "Come and gather together for the supper of the great God, that you may eat the flesh of kings and captains and mighty men and horses, and the flesh of people, free and slave, small and great." Sounds a little like chapter 6 — the big people and the little people — but anybody fighting against God will have to deal with the wrath of the Lamb.

But there is another supper, in verse 7 and verse 9: "Let us be glad and rejoice and give him glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come and his wife has made herself ready." Verse 9: "Blessed are those who are called to the marriage supper of the Lamb. These are the true sayings of God."

If you had a choice, we'd all want to go to the bridal supper of the Lamb at the end of time. A blessing is pronounced upon those who are invited. Yes, it's a day of reckoning, but it's a day of rejoicing. It means we're protected. God has called us up out of this world — the wedding feast of the Lamb, in the presence of God, with the bride, and you and I are the bride, given white clothing to say we are acceptable in God's presence.

The only ones invited to the other supper on the battlefield are the birds, and that's the one we want to avoid. Blessed are those invited to the marriage feast in heaven; and those who are not found at the marriage feast of the Lamb are the ones who will be found at the feast for the birds.

## Conclusion: Our Hope in the Lamb

We've been talking about God's Lamb. He takes away the sin of the world. He brings life by taking away that sin. And yet for those who don't want life — eternal life, resurrection life in God's time — they will share in the curse at the end of the age.

God gave his beloved Son as the sacrificial Lamb to take away your sin. God overcame our worst enemy when Jesus took up his life from the dead. Hebrews 2:14 talks about people who all their lives lived in fear of death. But Jesus rose from the dead after the crucifixion, and we have a part in that because he is our Lamb, our Lord and Savior, and he promised to raise us up also.

Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. And then in Revelation 19, behold the risen, exalted Lamb of God, who one day will take away sinners out of the world. Then it will be a better world — a renewed place, a new heaven and a new earth, when God brings judgment upon those who have been fighting against him all their lives.

We want to be at the wedding feast of the Lamb. Without the Lamb who was slain, we are no better than John the Baptist wasting in prison — never thought he would die like that. The axeman may be coming, but we are all going to face death. But with the risen Christ — with this risen Lamb who was slain — now saying, "I'm still in control, and I'm taking charge of the affairs at the end of the world" — we have hope in this life. Jesus sits at the right hand of God the Father, and that knowledge gives us assurance that he ever lives to make intercession for us.

We have hope now. But more than that, we have eternal life right now that gives us a future. A future not just of strumming a harp, but — as Revelation 5 says — we are going to reign with him. It will be an active, interesting life, a holy life in the presence of God.

And so we add our voices to the angels of heaven, and when we consider the Lamb who takes away the sin of the world, we say: worthy is the Lamb.
